nativeRange.habitat: Lowland streams, canals, ditches, and sluggish blackwater areas in peat swamp forests; typically found near the water surface in slow to moderately flowing waters with tannin-stained, acidic conditions

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, water sprite, floating plants)
driftwood and leaf litter to mimic blackwater biotope

diet.notes: Accepts high-quality dried flake and micro-pellets of appropriate size. In the wild feeds on small aquatic and terrestrial invertebrates and surface insects. Regular supplementation with small live or frozen foods (Daphnia, Artemia, bloodworm, micro-worm) improves coloration, overall condition, and breeding readiness. Avoid relying solely on dry food.

avoidWith: fish requiring hard alkaline water incompatible with soft acidic conditions

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo page for Rasbora tornieri (ID 22832). Includes a user-submitted photo by Ahsan Al Hidayat (Indonesia, Lampung, 2022, 9 cm specimen, CC BY-NC) and an FAO archival image from Cambodia.

summary: A larger SE Asian rasbora reaching ~13 cm, immediately recognised by the vivid yellow lobes of the caudal fin. An active, peaceful shoaler best kept in groups of six or more in a spacious, well-planted soft-water aquarium with a secure lid — a confirmed jumper.
careNotes: The Yellowtail Rasbora is one of the larger species in the genus, with FishBase recording specimens to 17 cm SL though aquarium-kept individuals typically reach 10–13 cm. Its most striking feature is the bright yellow coloration of the upper and lower caudal fin lobes, which gives the species its common name. A minimum tank length of 120 cm (approximately 225 L for a proper shoal) is strongly recommended; these are active, open-water swimmers that require horizontal swimming space. Keep in groups of at least six — smaller numbers result in skittish behaviour and increased stress. A tight-fitting lid is essential: the species is a confirmed jumper and escape risk is rated high. Water chemistry should be soft and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 4.0–7.0, gH 1–10), reflecting the blackwater peat-swamp and lowland stream environments of Indochina, Peninsular Malaysia, Sumatra, and Borneo. The species is reportedly adaptable to slightly harder or more neutral conditions in aquaria but thrives best in soft, tannin-stained water. Supplement the diet regularly with small live or frozen invertebrates (Daphnia, Artemia, bloodworm) alongside quality dried food. Maintain excellent water quality with efficient filtration and regular partial water changes; the species is intolerant of organic waste build-up. Compatible with other peaceful soft-water species of similar size. Avoid housing with fin-nippers or large aggressive fish.
breedingNotes: Successful captive breeding has not been documented in the hobby literature. The species is thought to be an egg scatterer that spawns without parental care, likely triggered by the onset of the rainy season in the wild. Eggs are reported to hatch within 18–48 hours and fry become free-swimming within a further 24–48 hours; newly free-swimming fry require microscopic first foods such as Paramecium or commercial liquid fry food before progressing to micro-worm and baby brine shrimp nauplii.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Rasbora tornieri
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided water parameters (temperature 20–26°C, pH 4.0–7.0, hardness 18–179 ppm), tank minimum surface area (120 × 30 cm), diet (small invertebrates, live/frozen supplementation), shoal minimum (6), native range (Central Sumatra, Cambodia/Mekong, Peninsular Malaysia, Borneo), habitat (flowing streams and blackwater peat swamp), and breeding notes (egg scatterer, no parental care, 18–48 h hatch).

additional.name: FishBase — Rasbora tornieri
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (17.0 cm SL), taxonomy (Cypriniformes / Danionidae / Rasborinae), native range (Indochina, Malaysia, Indonesia), habitat (streams, canals and ditches in lowland floodplains; surface-dwelling), diet (exogenous insects), IUCN Least Concern (2019). FishBase family listed as Danionidae; Cyprinidae used per project brief as Danionidae is treated as a subfamily of Cyprinidae in the broader hobby classification.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Yellowtail rasbora, Rasbora tornieri
additional.notes: Confirmed peaceful community temperament, minimum shoal of 6, minimum 225 L tank for a shoal, importance of tight-fitting lid (jumper), pH 6.5–7.5 and temperature 22.2–25.6°C noted as aquarium-practical range within the broader SeriouslyFish parameters. Diet: quality dried food plus daily live/frozen (Artemia, Daphnia, bloodworm).

additional.name: Aquadiction — Yellowtail Rasbora Care Guide
additional.notes: Supplementary hobbyist care guide. Confirmed tank setup preferences (heavily planted or river-style with rocks and moderate flow), substrate of gravel and smooth stones, open swimming space requirements, and hardness range 8–12 °dH cited for practical aquarium keeping.

fish.breedingNotes: No documented successful captive spawning. Presumed egg scatterer with no parental care; likely rainy-season triggered in the wild. Eggs reportedly hatch 18–48 h post-spawning; fry require Paramecium-sized first foods.
